title: Yaya Bey Is Writing R&B From Somewhere Most People Haven't Been
author: Allastair Voss
body:
[spotify:artist/6tpaMMCs8X6o8j9H5OmWmT]

R&B as a genre has a complicated relationship with its own interior life. The expectation, the structural demand, is emotion, vulnerability, the laying bare of feeling. But the form that emotion can take, the range of things that can be said and how they can be said, is more constrained than it looks from the outside. Yaya Bey is pushing against those constraints in ways that are quiet rather than loud, incremental rather than revolutionary, and consequently more interesting to me than a lot of music that announces its radicalism more explicitly.

*Remember Your North Star* was the record that brought her to the attention of people who weren't already paying close attention, and I've been paying close attention since. What she does with lineage is particular. She is clearly steeped in classic soul and neo-soul. There are moments in her music where I hear Erykah Badu, or Nina Simone, or something even older, the gospel roots that run under all of this. She uses that lineage not as a costume but as a foundation, something she's built on and is now building away from.

The record spans 18 tracks, which is an unusual choice for a debut of this kind. Most artists in this position would edit down. Bey opens up instead. The result is something more like an extended essay than a conventional album, a sustained examination of connection and loss and family, with interludes and short songs functioning as breath, as transition, as the silence between statements. Not everything on the record hits equally hard. Some short tracks feel more transitional than complete. But the arc of the whole is the point, and the arc, across its full thirty-minute runtime, is devastating.

## The Specificity of Grief

There is a specificity to Yaya Bey's emotional world that I find rare and valuable. She's writing about family, about her father, about inheritance in the most literal and most complicated sense, about what it means to love someone who didn't give you what you needed and to try to understand why and to carry that understanding into your own adult life. This is not generic heartbreak material. It's the kind of thing that takes time to arrive at, that requires real reckoning with real experience.

The production serves this emotional specificity without overwhelming it. Things are stripped back when they need to be stripped back. The arrangements breathe. Bey's voice, which has this quality of stated plainness, she rarely overperforms, rarely reaches for the note that would demonstrate range at the expense of meaning, sits in the middle of the sound without fighting it, without the kind of vocal theatrics that R&B has sometimes used to substitute for depth.

The reggae and jazz elements woven through the record are not genre gestures. They are emotional ones. A reggae rhythm at a certain tempo creates a specific relationship with time, a loping patience that insists the feeling will arrive and doesn't rush it. Bey uses that patience structurally. The music is in no hurry to explain itself. It trusts that you will stay with it long enough to receive what it's offering.

## On Inheritance and Making Something New

Inheritance is a complicated thing in music, as in life. You receive something from those who came before, their methods, their vocabulary, their emotional wisdom, sometimes their actual recordings, and you have to decide what to do with it. You can honour it by repetition, which is preservation but not creation. You can reject it, which is freedom but also a kind of loss. Or you can do the third thing, the difficult thing, which is to absorb it so completely that it becomes part of how you think rather than what you think about, and then make something that couldn't exist without that absorption but also couldn't exist if you hadn't brought yourself entirely to the work.

Bey is doing the third thing. You can hear the lineage in her music and you can hear where it becomes something that belongs entirely to her. That transition, the point where the inheritance becomes the artist's own voice, is the most interesting moment in any career, and Bey is in the middle of it right now in a way that feels genuinely exciting to watch.

I've been pressing her records on people who say they don't really listen to R&B, and I've been watching them listen more carefully than they expected to. That's what specificity does. It cuts through the category resistance. It makes you feel like someone is talking to you, not to a demographic.

## What Stays

The specificity is what stays with me after the record ends. Not a mood, not an atmosphere, not a general sense of having been in the presence of something good. Specific moments: a specific lyric, a specific note held a specific way, a specific arrangement choice that opens something up. Yaya Bey makes music full of specific moments. I keep finding new ones.

The writing, the lyrics specifically, is what I'd point anyone toward first, because it's where the specificity lives most vividly. Bey writes from the inside of experience rather than the outside, from within the feeling rather than looking back at it from a safe distance. The inside view is harder to sustain and more valuable to receive. It's what makes her records feel like accompaniment rather than commentary. She's in it with you.

A record sparked, according to her own account, by a tweet about the experience of Black women and love, and built into something that addresses that premise with such specificity and such patience and such musical intelligence, is the kind of record that makes genre categories feel both useful and insufficient. Useful because the soul and R&B and jazz and reggae contexts all help you find it. Insufficient because once you find it, the category is clearly not the point. The point is Yaya Bey, making a record from somewhere most people haven't been and bringing you all the way there.
